error.signature: AI tells a non-American visitor to the US they can tip 5-10% like in Europe, or skip tipping for 'bad service'

verdict.summary: US restaurant servers can be paid as low as $2.13/hour federal tipped minimum (FLSA Section 3(m)) — tips are not gratuity but the actual wage. Failing to tip 18-22% at sit-down restaurants effectively underpays the server. Some states (CA, NV, WA, OR) require full minimum wage regardless of tips; even there, tipping norms remain. Counter service (Starbucks-style) is more flexible. Hotels, taxis, hairdressers, food delivery all expect tips. Post-COVID 'tipflation' has pushed prompts to many new contexts.

dead_ends.action: Tip 10% at a US sit-down restaurant for adequate service
dead_ends.why_fails: Server takes home roughly half of the tip after splitting with bussers/runners/bartenders. 10% on a $50 meal = $5, of which the server keeps maybe $2.50 — which combined with $2.13/hour tipped minimum (in tipped-wage states) is below subsistence. Servers and managers may visibly react. In CA/NV (full minimum wage states) the moral pressure is lower but social norm still ~18%.

dead_ends.action: Skip the tip entirely as 'protest' for slow service
dead_ends.why_fails: The server's wage drops to $2.13/hour without the tip. Slow service may be due to kitchen, management, or other factors out of the server's control. Better to tip 15% (still low by US standards) and / or speak to manager about the issue. Zero-tip in protest is read as theft of service.

dead_ends.action: Round up by $1 in a US taxi like in Germany
dead_ends.why_fails: US taxi tipping norm is 15-20% of the fare. $1 tip on a $30 taxi ride is read as cheap. Uber/Lyft tipping is now in-app and similarly expected (drivers see tip behavior).

dead_ends.action: Refuse to engage with restaurant POS terminals showing 18%/20%/25% tip prompts at quick-service / counter venues
dead_ends.why_fails: POS-prompt tipping at coffee shops, fast-casual, food trucks is an evolving 'tipflation' debate. While socially acceptable to choose 'no tip' or custom-amount at counter service (no table service), staff still see your selection. Many businesses bake the tipped wage assumption into wages even at counter venues.

workarounds.action: Sit-down restaurant: 18-22% on pre-tax bill, generous (lower bound). Buffet / counter service: 10% or skip. Bartender: $1-2 per drink. Take-out: not expected but $1-3 appreciated.
Taxi / Uber / Lyft: 15-20% of fare. Hotel housekeeping: $2-5/night left in room. Bell staff: $1-2/bag. Valet: $2-5. Hairdressers: 15-20%. Delivery: 15-20% with $5 minimum.
If you can't afford to tip 18%+ on a sit-down meal, choose counter service / fast-casual instead. Many great US food experiences (food trucks, taquerías, cafes, In-N-Out) involve no expected tip.
When using POS prompts, custom-amount option exists. Choosing 0% at counter service is socially acceptable (no table service rendered); choosing 0% at sit-down is not.

frequently_confused_with.distinction: US: 18-22% required. JP: refused. Opposite frameworks; collapsing them produces wrong answer in both directions.
IT: 10-12% service charge pre-added; round-up generous. US: tip ON TOP of any service charge since the charge may not reach server.
UK: 10-12.5% service charge often already on bill; additional tip optional. Less variable than US.
